planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist, well-drained, humus-rich soil; seeds require cold stratification for 3 months before sowing. Aralia spreads by root suckers and will form a colony over time — site it where spreading is acceptable.

directions: Plant in moist to average, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade; Hercules' club is a striking native shrub or small tree with enormous compound leaves up to 4 feet long — among the largest leaves of any temperate North American woody plant — that give it a dramatic, tropical appearance in the summer garden. The thick, spiny stems and trunk give rise to both its common names. In late summer, large panicles of tiny white flowers attract hundreds of butterflies and bees; the small black berries that follow are eagerly consumed by mockingbirds, catbirds, robins, and over 30 other bird species. The plant produces root suckers freely and eventually forms a colony, which can be managed by removing unwanted shoots. A rare native plant for a bold tropical effect in naturalized or woodland-edge plantings.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist, acidic, humus-rich soil; swamp titi tolerates periodic flooding and grows naturally in bog margins, cypress swamps, and pitcher plant bogs of the Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain. Propagate from softwood cuttings in summer.

directions: Plant in wet to moist, acidic, peaty or humus-rich soil in full sun to light shade; swamp titi is a native Gulf and Atlantic Coastal Plain shrub of remarkable ornamental and ecological value that is still underused in landscape horticulture. The long, drooping racemes of small white honey-scented flowers in late spring and summer are among the most important nectar sources for honey production in the Florida Panhandle, where 'titi honey' is a regional specialty. The semi-evergreen to evergreen, leathery leaves turn vivid red and orange in fall before dropping. It tolerates flooding, poor acid soil, and full sun in swampy conditions — exactly the site conditions where most ornamental shrubs fail. In zone 9a, it makes an excellent native screen in wet or poorly drained areas.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring or fall in moist to wet, acidic, peaty or sandy soil; inkberry requires acidic conditions (pH 4.5–6.0) and is one of the most adaptable native shrubs for wet, seasonally flooded sites. Plant both male and female plants for berry production.

directions: Plant in moist to wet, acidic soil in full sun to partial shade; inkberry is a versatile native evergreen holly of the Atlantic and Gulf Coastal Plain with glossy dark green leaves and small black berries that provide essential winter food for over 35 bird species including bluebirds, robins, cedar waxwings, and hermit thrushes. Unlike many hollies, inkberry tolerates wet, seasonally flooded sites and even some saltwater intrusion, making it ideal for rain gardens, bioswales, and poorly drained sites where most shrubs fail. Numerous compact cultivars have been developed for landscape use, including 'Compacta' (3–4 ft), 'Shamrock' (3–4 ft, very cold hardy), and 'Nigra' (4–6 ft with good fruiting). Both male and female plants are needed for berry production; one male ('Nordic' or 'Strongbox') pollinates several females.

planting.note: Plant container stock in spring in moist but well-drained, humus-rich soil with the roots shaded and the stems in sun; woodbine tolerates partial shade better than Japanese honeysuckle and is preferred for informal hedgerows and native garden settings across Europe.

directions: Plant in moist, humus-rich, well-drained soil in full sun to partial shade, keeping the root zone cool and shaded while allowing stems to climb toward light; woodbine is the native European honeysuckle celebrated in Shakespeare's writings and the source of the intensely sweet evening fragrance associated with English cottage gardens. Unlike the invasive Japanese honeysuckle (L. japonica), this species is non-invasive and suitable for planting in naturalistic gardens. The tubular flowers, creamy-white flushed with pink and purple, are exceptionally fragrant in the evening and attract hawk moths; the red berries feed warblers, thrushes, and other birds in late summer. Popular cultivars include 'Serotina' (late Dutch, deep purple outside), 'Graham Thomas' (creamy white aging to yellow), and 'Belgica' (early Dutch honeysuckle). Prune lightly after flowering to shape.

planting.note: Plant tubers 2–3 inches deep in spring in well-drained soil; earth pea is easiest to establish from tubers rather than seed, which can be slow. The vine dies back in winter and re-emerges from the tubers in spring. Give stems something to climb.

directions: Plant tubers in well-drained, average to poor soil in full sun, giving the climbing stems support from a trellis, fence, or neighboring plants; earth pea is a remarkable perennial legume from central Europe that forms small, chestnut-flavored starchy tubers along its root system — the edible reward in addition to the ornamental rose-pink flowers. The tubers can be eaten raw, boiled, or roasted and have a pleasant, sweet, slightly floury flavor. Historically the plant was gathered as a famine food across rural Europe and was cultivated as a minor food crop in the Netherlands and France. Like other legumes, it fixes atmospheric nitrogen and gradually improves soil fertility. The vine is vigorous and twining, to about 3–6 feet, and the fragrant flowers attract bumblebees. An unusual, under-exploited edible perennial for food forest and permaculture plantings.
sources: Stephen Barstow - Around the World in 80 Plants
